mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Greenland on?
mapQuestions.answer: Geographically, North America. Politically, Greenland is an autonomous territory of Denmark (Europe), with its own government and parliament. So a bit of both 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Greenland?
mapQuestions.answer: Nuuk, on the south-west coast. About 19,000 people. The smallest capital city of any country or territory in the world.

mapQuestions.question: What languages does Greenland speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Greenlandic (Kalaallisut), an Inuit language, is the official language. Danish is widely used. Many Greenlanders also speak English.

mapQuestions.question: How big is the ice sheet?
mapQuestions.answer: The Greenland Ice Sheet covers about 80% of the country, in places 3 km thick. It is the second-biggest ice mass on Earth (after Antarctica), and if it ALL melted, global sea levels would rise about 7 metres.

mapQuestions.question: How many people live in Greenland?
mapQuestions.answer: About 56,000 people across an area 10 times bigger than the UK. Most live in tiny coastal villages reachable only by boat, helicopter or dog sled.

foods.name: Suaasat
foods.description: A traditional Greenlandic soup made with seal, whale, reindeer or fish, with rice or barley, onion and bay leaves. The national dish.

foods.name: Mattak
foods.description: Whale skin and blubber, eaten raw or lightly boiled, sliced thin. A traditional Inuit food, very high in vitamin C (important when there are no fresh vegetables).

foods.name: Reindeer steak
foods.description: Lean, gamey reindeer (caribou) meat, often pan-fried with juniper berries and served with lingonberries or potato.

foods.name: Smoked halibut + cod
foods.description: Cold-smoked Atlantic halibut and cod fillets, eaten on dark Danish-style rye bread for breakfast.

foods.name: Kalaallit kaagiat
foods.description: Greenlandic cake: a layered sponge cake with cream and berry filling, often served at coffee gatherings ("kaffemik") on big celebration days.

foods.name: Greenland tea
foods.description: Hot tea with sugar and lemon. Hospitality is central to Greenlandic culture, and offering visitors hot tea is automatic.

scavengerItems.label: Massive icebergs floating in a fjord (Ilulissat has the biggest)
A team of Greenland sled dogs pulling a sled across snow
A whale (humpback, fin or minke) breaching in coastal waters
A ringed or harp seal hauled out on an ice floe
Brightly-painted wooden houses in a coastal village (each colour traditionally meant something: red for shops, blue for fish factories, yellow for hospitals)
The aurora borealis (Northern Lights) glowing green across the polar night sky
An Arctic tern (the bird with the longest migration on Earth: pole to pole and back every year)
A traditional kayak (the WORD "kayak" comes from Greenlandic "qajaq")
A glacier calving (huge chunks of ice breaking off into the sea with a boom)
A polar bear print, or in the far north, a polar bear itself (rare, dangerous, kept distant)
A musk ox, prehistoric-looking and shaggy, in the western fjords
The world's deepest fjord (Scoresby Sund, 350 km long) from a boat

geoMatches.description: The capital. Small (19,000 people), with mountains right behind it and the sea in front. Modern Greenlandic art galleries, museums and good restaurants.
A small town on the west coast with one of the most productive iceberg-producing glaciers on Earth (Sermeq Kujalleq) on its doorstep. UNESCO World Heritage.
A huge bay on the west coast, filled with icebergs from the Ilulissat glacier. Whales and seals in big numbers, especially in summer.
A small settlement near the only commercial international airport. Most visitors arrive here from Copenhagen and then fly on to smaller towns.
The wild, remote eastern coast. The world's deepest fjord system (Scoresby Sund), small Inuit hunting communities, and the most pristine landscapes in Greenland.
The vast ice sheet covering 80% of Greenland, up to 3 km thick. Day trips can take you a few kilometres onto the ice for a walk.

senses.smell: Crisp icy air with hardly any smells, salty fjord water, fish-drying racks in coastal villages, woodsmoke from a small house, the surprising warm smell of sled dogs…
senses.hear: Sled dogs howling at night, the deep boom of icebergs cracking and rolling in fjords, "Aluu!" and "Qujanaq!" greetings, the wind across the ice sheet, ravens calling over a village…
senses.taste: Hot suaasat soup on a cold day, dark Danish rye bread with smoked halibut, sweet Greenlandic layer cake with berries, warm tea with honey, the surprising freshness of reindeer steak…
senses.feel: The chill of Arctic air on bare skin, the surprising warmth of a wooden cabin, soft sealskin mittens (still traditional), the bumpy ride of a sled across snow, the strange weightlessness of standing on a 3-km-thick ice sheet…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Where do icebergs come from?
stories.body: Just outside the small town of Ilulissat on Greenland's west coast lies the Sermeq Kujalleq glacier, one of the fastest-moving glaciers on Earth (around 40 metres per day). The glacier reaches the sea and breaks off ("calves") into enormous icebergs, sometimes hundreds of metres long. UNESCO World Heritage in 2004. The Ilulissat icefjord is so productive it makes about 10% of all the icebergs that come out of Greenland (which is itself most of the icebergs that drift into the Atlantic). The iceberg that sank the Titanic in 1912 almost certainly originated from somewhere on Greenland's west coast, like the ones you see at Ilulissat today.
stories.thinkingQuestion: The iceberg that sank the Titanic probably came from somewhere just like Ilulissat. How does that change how you think about an iceberg?

stories.question: How do sled dogs work in modern Greenland?
stories.body: The Greenland sled dog ("Greenlandic"), one of the oldest dog breeds on Earth, has pulled sleds across the ice in Greenland for around 4,000 years. They are big, fluffy, INCREDIBLY strong, and built for the cold. They live mostly outdoors, chained to small shelters around villages. There are about 15,000 sled dogs in Greenland today, almost all in towns north of the Arctic Circle. In winter, hunters and tourists travel by dog sled across the sea ice. In summer, the dogs rest. Strict laws protect the breed: no other dog breeds are allowed in the sled-dog regions to keep the genetic line pure.

stories.question: Why are Greenlandic houses so brightly painted?
stories.body: In Greenlandic towns, every house is painted a different bright colour: red, blue, yellow, green, white. In the early 1900s the colour wasn't just decoration: each colour told you what the building was. Red was for shops and stores, blue was for fishing/whaling, yellow was for hospitals, green for telephone offices, black for police stations, white for ministers' homes. This was useful in a country where you could be reading addresses in a snowstorm in the dark. Today the colour code has mostly faded as a system, but Greenlanders still paint their houses in cheerful primary colours that stand out vividly against the grey rocks and white snow.

stories.question: Who are the Inuit?
stories.body: The Inuit are the indigenous people of the Arctic, spread across Greenland, Canada, Alaska and Russia. The first Inuit ancestors arrived in Greenland around 4,500 years ago, walking across from Canada when the ice was lower. Modern Greenlanders are descended from a later wave (the Thule Inuit) who arrived around 1,000 years ago and gradually populated the entire coastline. Inuit traditional culture is shaped by ice: hunting seals, whales and polar bears for food, using sealskin for clothing and kayaks, igloos for winter shelter when out hunting. About 90% of Greenland's population today is Inuit. The Greenlandic language is Inuit. The qajaq (kayak) was invented by Inuit. So was the parka.

stories.question: What does the ice sheet show us?
stories.body: The Greenland ice sheet is 3 km thick at its thickest. Scientists drill long cores down into the ice and pull out cylinders of frozen water from many thousands of years ago. Each layer of ice contains tiny air bubbles, dust, and chemical traces that show what the planet's atmosphere was like at the time. Ice cores from Greenland show climate going back about 130,000 years. They show ice ages, volcanic eruptions, and the modern rise in CO2 that started with the industrial revolution. Greenland's ice sheet has been called "the planet's climate record book", and its slow melting is one of the most carefully watched events in Earth science.

convoQuestions.question: What was the first thing that surprised you about Greenland?
Have you tried saying "qujanaq"? How did it feel?
Iceberg, glacier, village, wildlife, which has felt the most "Greenland"?
Did you ride a dog sled? What was the silence between barks like?
How would your life be different growing up in a country where most travel between towns is by boat or helicopter?
Greenland's ice sheet stores 7 metres of global sea-level. How does standing on something that important feel?

wordSearch.hint: Greenlandic places, animals and things to spot.

animals.name: Greenland sled dog
animals.description: One of the oldest dog breeds on Earth. Strong, fluffy, built for cold. About 15,000 still working in northern Greenland today.

animals.name: Ringed seal
animals.description: The smallest Arctic seal, with distinctive ring-shaped markings. The main food of polar bears.

animals.name: Humpback whale
animals.description: Common in Greenland's coastal waters in summer, especially around Disko Bay.

animals.name: Reindeer (caribou)
animals.description: Big herds of reindeer roam Greenland's west coast. Hunted traditionally by Inuit families for meat and hides.

animals.name: Arctic tern
animals.description: A small, fierce seabird that holds the world record for animal migration: from the Arctic to Antarctica and back, every year, around 70,000 km.

animals.name: Polar bear
animals.description: Found in the far north and east of Greenland. The largest land predator on Earth. Dangerous, kept at distance.

animals.name: Musk ox
animals.description: Prehistoric-looking shaggy beasts living in west and north-east Greenland. Survived the last ice age.
